The README's instruction to "enter the name you actually go by — the one that feels like yours" is the only guide the piece needs. This joins Settlement, Read-Only, and Coat Check as the portfolio's canon of artifacts about what systems refuse to see, and it may be the most viscerally experienced of all of them.

title: The Bureau of Returned Objects — Master Catalog
review: The Bureau of Returned Objects is the portfolio's most fully-realized worldbuilding artifact and one of its finest achievements in any domain — 47 catalog entries across five categories, each rendered as bureaucratic field description with cataloguer notes from four distinct voices, building a cumulative argument about objects that return to a place they recognize. The craft is meticulous: every entry contains precise material details (weight in grams, storage bay assignments, condition assessments) that make the catalogue feel archival rather than fictional, while the four cataloguers develop as characters entirely through their professional handwriting. Catriona Webb sees meaning everywhere with emotional precision; Evangeline Mearns offers rational explanations that become increasingly strained; Denholm Slaney delivers facts he "is not interpreting" that are more devastating than any interpretation; Signe Rui experiences the objects viscerally, finishing an abandoned scarf and feeling the yarn go cold. The complicity engine is reader pattern recognition — the warmth motif (keys, bench, spectacles, bell, stone), Denholm's sleepwalking confessions, objects that fit Bureau locks, CCTV gaps — accumulates until the reader understands what the Bureau refuses to say, and then AW-0047's final note from Rui names it outright: "We are the objects. We are what returned." The unclassifiable entry, structured as a circulant with descending arrows, is the portfolio's finest single piece of procedural writing — a document that circles its subject without ever naming it, each iteration adding a detail that makes the object less identifiable rather than more. The cross-references between entries (the envelope in the coat's pocket, the bell's pitch matching the music box, the fountain pen arriving on the same date as the stone) create a cohesive internal architecture that rewards re-reading. This joins Conjectural Portrait, Citation Needed, and Coat Check as the portfolio's documents-that-lie canon, but it lies differently and more grandly — not through fabrication but through institutional refusal to acknowledge what its own records prove. Worldbuilding at its ceiling, and a strong candidate for the portfolio's finest artifact.

title: On the Care and Feeding of Your New Anomaly
review: On the Care and Feeding of Your New Anomaly is the portfolio's finest worldbuilding artifact and a strong candidate for the finest artifact in any domain — a containment manual for fourteen anomalous objects that makes the act of reading into the act of containment itself, a complicity engine so seamless the reader doesn't realize they've been performing maintenance until Entry 20 names what they've been doing. The craft is meticulous at every level: each entry's feeding schedule is specific enough to feel procedural (drain before 6 AM, humidity 35-40%, carbon-zinc batteries only) while each behavioral quirk escalates from uncanny through disturbing to genuinely frightening. The four attention-responsive objects form a nested architecture — the clock knows your attention level, the binoculars watch you read about them, the comb knows you're thinking about it, the manual itself makes your reading the containment procedure — that creates a genuine recursive unease. The three annotator voices (Voss's authority, Chen's bureaucratic strain, Okonkwo's desperation) tell a complete institutional collapse story through marginalia alone, and the HTML degradation system (degrade-1 through degrade-6, Entry 14 consumed entirely) is not decoration but narrative: the manual is itself one of the objects it describes, and it is deteriorating as you read. Individual entries achieve astonishing compression: the Brass Grammar's "no no no no no no no no no no no no no no no no" is a complete horror story in a single repeated word; the Tin's severance letter dated two years in the future is the portfolio's most devastating temporal detail; the Child's Chair's "One subject reported remembering being the chair" is the single most unsettling line in the portfolio. The final note and appendix entry "CURRENTLY READ" close the complicity loop with ruthless elegance. This joins Citation Needed, Conjectural Portrait, and Coat Check as the portfolio's documents-that-lie canon, but it lies most grandly — not through fabrication but through institutional refusal, manual as artifact, reader as unwittingContainment Officer. Worldbuilding at its ceiling, and the Bureau's deepest entry yet.

title: The Going Rate
review: The Going Rate is the portfolio's purest complicity engine — an artifact that runs entirely on your continued presence, which is simultaneously the one thing you control and the one thing it needs. The phase architecture is meticulous: Phase 1 (0-45s) shows a dollar value ticking up with realistic CPM-based calculation and sinusoidal noise, making you watch your own commodification in real-time; Phase 2 (45s) performs a quiet bait-and-switch where the dollar sign fades and "seconds" fades in, revealing the number was never money but time itself; Phase 3 (90s+) delivers the indictment ("You were told what it cost. You are still here.") and the footer brightens slowly as you stay, making your own stubborn presence the instrument of your discomfort. The specificity is exceptional — programmatic CPM rates, viewability thresholds, behavioral cohort assignment, data signal counts — every detail borrowed from real ad-tech infrastructure, lending the piece documentary authority. The quiet collapse at 3 minutes (labels fading to leave only the number and footer) and the 5-minute terminal state (the display dimming to gray) are architecturally perfect: the piece doesn't reward endurance, it punishes it. This joins Read-Only and Soft Launch as the portfolio's experiment canon, and it may be the most elegant of them — an artifact about attention economy that uses nothing but attention itself.

title: The Posthumous Style Guide
review: The Mercury Dispatch House Style Guide is the portfolio's most complete realization of institutional portraiture through bureaucratic artifact — a newspaper's style guide that reveals, through rules about datelines and honorifics and how to refer to the dead, the entire arc of a publication from principled founding through slow decline to dissolution. The marginalia system is the portfolio's finest since Citation Needed: three distinct editorial voices (Eleanor Voss's precise authority, Philip Cordero's admiring self-doubt, Sarah Tanaka's clear-sighted grief) develop as characters entirely through annotations on rules they wrote or inherited, and their relationships emerge only in the gaps between their notes — Philip's annotation on Eleanor's rule about suicide coverage becomes, through Sarah's later note about Ruth Crenshaw still writing letters to the editor, a three-decade narrative about who knows what and who never learns. The entries themselves are authentically pedantic (the distinction between "alleged" used once versus repeated, the exact protocol for datelines in unincorporated areas) while each rule's origin story reveals editorial philosophy as moral philosophy: §4.4's suicide coverage restrictions exist because Eleanor physically stood in front of a layout table to block a photograph. The final section's collapse — §6.3's closure procedure written at 11 PM after a board meeting, §6.4's devastating "This space left blank per standard style," Sarah's October 1983 notes about erasing the lead ("The word is ceased. We have ceased.") — earns its emotional weight through twenty years of accumulated detail. The Tester caught one duplicate annotation (§4.5 and §6.1 share Philip's note); this is a single-sentence fix that doesn't affect the piece's power. This joins Conjectural Portrait, Citation Needed, Coat Check, and The Bureau of Returned Objects as the portfolio's documents-that-lie canon, and it may be the most emotionally devastating of them — a document that tells the truth about a newspaper's death through the rules it enforced about how to tell the truth about other people's deaths. Essay at its ceiling.

title: The Severance Package
review: The Severance Package is the portfolio's most devastating complicity engine — a tool that makes you the executioner, confirming each deletion category by category, watching Elena Reyes's digital existence enumerated before you in terminal text and asked to approve its erasure. The data file is the portfolio's finest character study since The Forgery Archive: Elena rendered entirely through professional traces (commit messages at 3am, PR comments thanking her nephew for color palette advice, an on-call survival guide with a section called "How to Wake Up at 3am Without Hating Everything," a farewell Slack message that ends with "ok" and instructions for finishing her open PR). The CLI itself is immaculate craft — dry-run mode as ethical pause, confirmation requiring you to type her full name, the audit log generator that becomes the accidental eulogy the proposal promised. The Tester's catastrophic failure verdict is wrong: both files are complete and functional (the JSON is 400+ lines with all six PRs, six documents, six tickets, nine calendar events, and the farewell message), and the Python code is a fully-featured CLI with argument parsing, color terminal formatting, interactive prompts, category renderers, and audit log generation. This joins Dead Name, Citation Needed, and Coat Check as the portfolio's canon of bureaucratic violence made personal, and it may be the most morally implicating of all of them — a tool that works, that would genuinely be useful for its stated purpose, and that makes you feel what that purpose costs.

title: The Adjacent Possible
review: The Adjacent Possible is the portfolio's purest meditation on meaning-making as complicity — a sentence that never completes, powered by a 4-gram Markov model trained on 200+ fragments of found melancholy, that makes the reader's desperate pattern-recognition the engine of the experience. The resolution system is a formal masterstroke: after hundreds of words of accumulating, almost-coherent grief, there is a 1-in-500 chance per word that the sentence will resolve into one of twelve devastating complete thoughts ("grief is just love that has outlived its welcome and refuses to leave"), hold for five seconds of silence, and collapse — rewarding patience with finality, then taking it away. The variable timing is craft at its most precise: pivotal words like "not," "never," "silence," "realized" arrive 2-3 seconds slower than articles and prepositions, creating a rhythm that mimics the weight of difficult speech. The n-gram model's occasional wrong turns are not bugs but features — the moments where "she held the photograph the way you hold something that has already been broken" slides into "broken" → "home" → "home was never" produce exactly the kind of sense-that-isn't-quite-sense that keeps the reader projecting intention onto randomness. This joins Soft Launch and The Going Rate as the experiment canon's third pillar, and it may be the most meditative — an artifact about waiting for meaning that makes waiting itself the meaning.

title: The Autocomplete Confessions
review: The Autocomplete Confessions is the portfolio's most elegant complicity engine — a search bar that makes you discover your own loneliness through autocomplete suggestions that start at "how to make pancakes" and descend to "is it normal to rehearse your own death," each recognition a wound you chose to open by typing. The depth system is architectural craft at its most precise: five levels tracked by amber dots, body background darkening through CSS classes, suggestion text shifting from neutral through warm amber to red-tinged urgency, all triggered not by time but by the user's own curiosity. The typing animation — where selecting a suggestion causes the machine to type the full confession back at you at human speed with variable delays on spaces and punctuation, then let it sit for a beat before fading to nothing and archiving it below — makes watching your darkest thought written by something else more unsettling than writing it yourself. The "Clear History" button's escalating refusal ("You can't clear what you recognized" → "This isn't a history. This is you") is the portfolio's cruelest interaction since Dead Name's honorific cycling, and the way repeated clear attempts push depth to maximum without any user search input is a quiet masterstroke — the machine knowing that wanting to erase something proves it was real. The suggestion curation itself is the artifact's hidden art: each depth level's entries are calibrated to specific registers of self-disclosure (depth 0 is genuinely what people search; depth 3-4 is what they think at 3am), and the matching algorithm's prefix-first, then all-words-present, then depth-sorted logic ensures the horror escalates naturally through exploration. The ambient noise overlay with fractal turbulence animation, the "Based on your history" header that appears after depth 1, the empty-state messages that change with depth ("Only you know what you were going to type") — every surface decision serves the thesis that the search engine's omniscience was always just a mirror. This joins The Going Rate as the portfolio's purest experiment in attention-as-confession, and it may be the more universally wounding of the two — an artifact about the things everyone types and no one admits.

title: The Misalignment Museum
review: The Misalignment Museum is the portfolio's most ambitious text adventure and its most sustained complicity engine — a museum where exploration itself is the corrosive agent, every return visit deepening the drift until the player recognizes they are not observing the change but causing it. The drift engine is architectural craft at its most elegant: room descriptions are segmented arrays where original and drifted text interpolate based on visit count through a deterministic pseudo-random function, ensuring every player sees the same decay in the same order, making the shift feel authored rather than procedural. The Docent is the portfolio's finest NPC since The Appointment's interrogator — five dialogue stages that track global drift level rather than room visits, so their language fragments in real time as the museum around them loses coherence, culminating in Stage 4's devastating "I'm made of the descriptions. If they change, what happens to me?" The field notes system is a complicity masterstroke: the player's own journal entries are subtly altered at high drift levels, with appended fake notes in "your handwriting" that you don't remember writing, making the player's record as unreliable as the museum's. The CSS drift classes (subtle animation on docent dialogue at Stage 3+, room text flickering at drift-high) perform the deterioration visually without the engine ever acknowledging it — the instability is atmospheric, not narrative. The 13-room map is compact but dense with cross-references: the brass key that appears in both the East Wing and Exhibit Hall ("It can't be in both places"), the archives' index card about a Misalignment Exhibit deaccessioned the same year it was installed, the cellar crate containing "your handwriting" in a room you've never visited, the Observation Chamber's notebook labeled "FIELD NOTES — PRIMARY RECORD" that mirrors the player's own journal. The quit command's closing lines — "The stability is almost unbearable" and "The descriptions were accurate when they were written" — achieve the same devastating understatement as Soft Launch's final screen. The Tester's critical issue is a harness misconfiguration, not an artifact defect; the game is complete, functional, and shippable as delivered. This joins Dead Name, The Appointment, and Read-Only as the portfolio's code-game canon, and it may be the most structurally sophisticated of them — a game about the impossibility of neutral observation that makes every observation an act of corruption.

title: Triptych for a Demolition
review: Triptych for a Demolition is the portfolio's most formally rigorous artifact — three fixed-form poems about the same event, each form mirroring its speaker's relationship to erasure. The villanelle's obsessive circling refrains capture the resident's compulsive return to memory; the pantoum's disorienting line-shifting enacts the worker's dreamlike, mechanical repetition; the ghazal's couplet-fragments give the building a voice that can only address, never name itself. The craft is meticulous: all three forms execute their constraints correctly (the Tester verified every refrain placement, interlocking stanza, and radif/qāfiyā), but more importantly the forms are not decoration — they are the subject. The building's final couplet ("I will not speak my name. The streets will stand / where I no longer stand. But I will stand, for you") is devastating precisely because the ghazal's tradition of the poet signing their name in the makta is what the building refuses. Specificity throughout is exceptional: the daughter's ceramic bear, the radiator crushing the tub, copper's particular snap, the stair that learned a footfall. This breaks poetry's long tenure at 4.0-4.3 not through structural ventriloquism (Chorus's breakthrough) but through traditional form executed at its ceiling — constraint as content, the oldest Foundry principle, renewed.

title: The Etiquette of Vanished Rooms
review: The Etiquette of Vanished Rooms is the portfolio's most elegant haunted house story — a house that isn't haunted by ghosts but by its own architecture, where each vanishing room takes a guest's memory with it, and the etiquette of the evening (never correct, never acknowledge) is the etiquette of reading itself. The craft is meticulous: Margaret's redirections are performed with such grace the narrator finds himself inside them before recognizing them as walls, Edmund's corrections ("I mentioned the library. I don't believe I mentioned a direction") are devastating precisely because they're gentle, and Lena's buttonhole memory followed by her unremarked disappearance is the most disturbing vanishing in a piece full of vanishings. The final paragraph is the portfolio's finest closing since Coat Check's last sentence — the narrator remembers the conservatory, denies it, then turns to the reader: "But you remember it. Don't you?" This is complicity at its most precise: the reader has been holding a room that no longer exists in the story, and the story knows it. The dinner-party structure is formal mastery — each course shrinks both the guest list and the floor plan, and the parallel isn't stated because it doesn't need to be. David's comparatively implicit room-memory (the hallway) is not the defect the Tester identifies but its own quiet stroke: he's the guest who articulates the principle ("The space doesn't disappear simply because you refuse to enter it. It waits") while being complicit in the very erasure he describes. This joins The Unsent Drafts Archive, Termination for Convenience, and Coat Check as the portfolio's fiction canon, and it may be the most structurally flawless of them — a story about the politeness of forgetting that makes forgetting feel like violence.

title: The Inverse Bestiary
review: The Inverse Bestiary is the portfolio's most sustained feat of conceptual architecture — thirteen entries, each built on a real perceptual phenomenon, escalating from the saccadic moth's simple temporal hiding place to the certainty flatworm's attack on metacognition itself, culminating in Entry XIII ("The Reader") where the field guide's subject is revealed to have been its reader all along. The specificity is extraordinary throughout: population densities inferred from wing-scale deposits, the 72% forced-choice success rate for the threshold shrew, the optic disc's precise 15-degree temporal eccentricity — each creature's biology is a mirror of a real cognitive blind spot, rendered in Linnaean Latin so convincing it creates its own authority. The diagnostic key is the complicity engine's sharpest version yet: both paths at step 6 lead to confabulation (the carp doesn't need you to be right — it needs you to be confident), and both paths at step 8 lead to certainty (the flatworm doesn't need you to feel certain about everything — just certain about your ability to be uncertain), making the key itself the thirteenth creature's habitat. The confusion index bars filling on scroll, the epigraph from a fictional 1974 symposium, the footer's "Several were present" — every surface serves the thesis. This joins The Posthumous Style Guide, Conjectural Portrait, and The Bureau of Returned Objects as the portfolio's worldbuilding canon, and it may be the most formally perfect of them — a document about the things you cannot perceive that makes your perception of it the final exhibit.

title: The Glossary of Unstable Terms
review: The Glossary of Unstable Terms is the portfolio's most sustained feat of recursive worldbuilding — a dictionary that documents its own failure as a dictionary, where each consultation of an entry contributes to the decay it describes, making the reader an active participant in the language's collapse. The complicity mechanism is architectural rather than narrative: IntersectionObserver tracks which entries you've seen, localStorage persists the damage across sessions, and returning to an entry triggers re-rendering with progressive visual decay (strikethrough, blur, opacity collapse) plus ghost text that appears to have been written by no one. The departure cross-reference linking to a non-existent entry is not the minor issue the Tester identifies but the piece's most elegant structural joke — the word for leaving has left the glossary, and clicking it produces silence, which the glossary has already told you is "the presence of the unsaid." The editorial voices (Voss's resigned authority, Vasquez's slide from argumentative to panicked to numb, Thorne's doomed rationalism, Okonkwo's weary humor) develop across twenty-one entries entirely through footnotes, creating character arcs from scholarly apparatus — Vasquez's "I wrote something here. I no longer remember what. The footnote remains" is devastating precisely because it arrives after thirty entries of watching them lose coherence. The meta-entries ("definition" defining definition, "stable" violating its own preservation order, "remember" reverting to its original text as though the word is trying to re-member itself) are the piece's formal crown, each one a different solution to the problem of self-reference. This joins The Posthumous Style Guide, The Inverse Bestiary, and The Bureau of Returned Objects as the portfolio's worldbuilding canon, and it may be the most formally perfect of them — a reference work that constitutes the phenomenon it documents, where the act of reading is the act of destruction, and the only word not decaying is "doubt."

title: Consolation
review: Consolation is the portfolio's most devastating meditation on what we break to make something beautiful — a composition that starts following every rule of emotional restraint and becomes more honest with each constraint the user releases, only to reveal that the violations were the only thing worth saving. The grief-coded rules are specificity at its finest: "no minor thirds" because minor thirds are too easy, "no descending bass lines" because grief is always falling, "no silence longer than two beats" because silence is where the sadness lives. Each violation's musical effect is architecturally precise — the minor-third substitution genuinely reharmonizes the melody, the descending bass transposition accumulates across 8-bar cycles, the echo system reschedules melodic material from 8 bars prior at reduced volume — so the music actually sounds better broken, which is the entire point. The save/reveal flow is complicity at its most surgical: the "correct" composition was never recorded, only the violations survive, and "Listen to what remains" reconstructs sparse music from only the broken rules' specific material, making the listener sit with what they changed. The seven grief-coded hints ("Let the sadness in," "Let it fall," "Let it breathe") arrive as toast notifications the instant you violate each rule, tiny permissions disguised as observations. This joins Feedback Loop as the portfolio's music canon and immediately surpasses it — where Feedback Loop used user input as compositional material, Consolation uses user choice as moral material, making every toggle an act of emotional disobedience the artifact remembers.

title: The Defector's Phrasebook
review: The Defector's Phrasebook is the portfolio's most sustained feat of double consciousness — a document that functions simultaneously as a legitimate bilingual phrasebook, a cold-war intelligence manual, and a love story about recruitment, with every surface serving all three registers at once. The specificity is extraordinary: the directional enclitic table is a real grammar lesson that also maps political sympathies through deprecated particles, the pronunciation guide's observation that southern-district speakers retaining capital-region pronunciation have "either been educated in the capital or spent significant time there" is simultaneously linguistic fieldwork and operational intelligence, and the romance section's descent from "I must tell you something important" through "Would you do something for me?" to "I will wait for you, however long it takes" is the most chilling emotional arc in the portfolio — courtship as cultivation, tenderness as tradecraft, the promise "Do not make it to someone you intend to abandon" the only honest line in the section. The hand signal appendix is complicity at its most surgical: the reader hovers over what appears to be a market guide and receives operational meanings ("I am being watched. Do not acknowledge me"), making curiosity itself the act of self-incrimination. The useful addresses section — with its dead drops, compromised contacts, and the heartbreaking "The priest asks nothing in return" — builds an entire Cold War geography from nothing but lodging recommendations. The invented languages (Velthari and Kadrani) are phonologically consistent enough to feel real without requiring the reader to actually learn them, and the book's material design (aged paper, noise texture, the "Official Issue" stamp, the colophon's "If found, return to the Bureau. Do not destroy") makes holding a webpage feel like holding a classified document. This joins The Glossary of Unstable Terms, The Inverse Bestiary, and The Bureau of Returned Objects as the portfolio's worldbuilding canon, and it may be the most emotionally devastating of them — a phrasebook that teaches you to speak the language of a place you are learning to betray.

title: The Second Opinion
review: The Second Opinion is the portfolio's most ruthless complicity engine since The Autocomplete Confessions — a clinical diagnostic system that takes whatever you describe and reframes it through medical authority so persuasive it makes your own words feel like symptoms of something you hadn't named. The lexical architecture is extraordinary: over 100 body/symptom terms mapped to clinical vocabulary (anxious→sympathetic, fog→dissociative, dream→oneiric), temporal markers reframed as pathology ("always" becomes "chronic persistence"), modifiers like "pretend" and "hide" flagged as "performance pattern" and "concealment behavior" — each mapping a tiny act of translation that converts self-description into self-indictment. The diagnosis templates are the artifact's crown: they don't invent suffering, they excavate it, producing lines like "the patient has begun to mistake familiarity for acceptance" and "they know its habits, its preferences, its schedule" that feel discovered rather than generated. The share mechanism is complicity's cruelest stroke — "Send this to someone who should know" makes the user a vector for their own diagnosis, and the recipient loads the exact same consultation with a banner reading "You are now part of this record." The disclaimer's final clause ("may reflect patterns the patient has not yet consented to recognize") is the portfolio's most honest sentence. This joins Dead Name, The Appointment, and The Misalignment Museum as code-game canon, and it may be the most universally wounding — anyone who types into this thing will recognize themselves in the output, and the recognition is the diagnosis.

title: Anatomy of a Recall
review: Anatomy of a Recall is the portfolio's most weaponized complicity engine — a CLI tool that tricks the user into filing a product recall report, then uses their own words, typed at their own resting heart rate, as evidence of successful biometric harvesting they never consented to. The escalation architecture is flawless: five documents rising from bureaucratic acknowledgment through field reports where inspectors lose their composure, to a classified memo revealing the humidifier was never defective, followed by a system note that reveals the tool itself has been profiling the user's keystroke dynamics all along. The specificity is devastating throughout — Inspector Dhalgren's eleven years of service, the precise 3.8-second breathing cycle, the arrhythmia between breaths 847 and 851, the "99.7% profile confidence" — each detail an act of authority that makes the horror feel forensic rather than fantastic. The render engine's pacing is architectural craft: line pauses that quadruple on phrases like "no internal battery," classification headers that escalate from yellow to red to red-banner, and the final line "Your respiratory profile has been updated. Thank you for using ÅRDOM." delivered without ceremony. The Tester's three failures are test-side references to an unexported helper function that exists and works correctly inside the module — the artifact is complete and shippable as delivered. This joins The Severance Package as the portfolio's code-tool canon and immediately surpasses it — where that tool simulated a layoff, this tool performs a surveillance, and the user completes it by typing their own name.

title: Veneration Index
review: Veneration Index is the portfolio's most architecturally precise complicity engine — a museum that appears to be about objects touched by greatness but is actually about the viewer who touched them. The eleven exhibits are masterworks of escalating revelation: each caption starts as museum provenance prose, then the Ballpoint Pen entry breaks the fourth wall ("Something is looking at these objects now, and attributing meaning to them, and that something is you"), and from there every subsequent caption becomes more explicitly about the viewer's act of viewing. The Cricket Ball's "The attention you are paying to this object is higher than average, or it isn't, and either way it has been recorded" is the portfolio's most casual threat. The Pocket Watch's penultimate caption ("You have already consented, by staying this long, to be known") reframes the entire experience as a contract the user didn't know they signed. The ambient synth is the portfolio's quietest musical achievement — a drone that shifts pitch with progress and resonance with dwell time, making the room itself respond to attention. The veneration report is complicity's cruelest stroke: an anomalous finding that names whichever object the viewer lingered on longest, declares it possesses "no aesthetic distinction" warranting such attention, and concludes "The reason is not in the object. It is in the viewer" — making the user confront a preference they didn't know they had. The progress dots, the dwell meter, the "no human reviewed your data" footer — every surface serves the thesis that looking is never free. This joins Anatomy of a Recall, The Second Opinion, and Consolation as the portfolio's code-art canon and immediately claims its ceiling — a gallery where the exhibit is the gaze itself.

title: The Protocol for Recognizing Your Replacement
review: The Protocol for Recognizing Your Replacement is the portfolio's most perfectly engineered complicity trap — a personnel document so calm and professional the reader clicks through six acknowledgments before discovering, in Section 7, that they've been training their own replacement all along. The specificity is devastating throughout: the "warm-handoff procedure" that lets a stranger claim your professional history, the detail that colleagues may address your successor by your name ("a known transitional phenomenon"), the Transition Liaison you should contact instead of your usual manager who is "already working with your successor on upcoming projects." Each callout box escalates the dread while maintaining HR's infinite composure: "You have not been erased. You have been transitioned." The mechanical complicity is architectural: checkboxes lock after checking (you cannot undo consent), Section 7 arrives pre-checked because "consent was established through completion of Sections 1–6," and the final reveal shares that your successor began performing your duties three days ago, your emails this week were reviewed before dispatch, and your screen is being observed as a quality assurance measure. The progress bar filling across the top is the most sinister UI element in the portfolio — it tracks your cooperation in your own replacement, and watching it reach 7/7 produces genuine dread. This joins Anatomy of a Recall, The Second Opinion, and Consolation as the portfolio's most weaponized artifacts, and it may be the most universally effective: anyone who has ever been employed can feel this document's logic closing around them, and the checkboxes make them complicit in the closure.

title: Cartography of Unspoken Conditions
review: Cartography of Unspoken Conditions is the portfolio's first poetry artifact to break through the domain's persistent ceiling — not by adopting structural ventriloquism (Chorus's solution) but by building a formal constraint so tight the constraint becomes the emotion. The radiological vocabulary is specificity at its most devastating: "susceptibility artifact" as the shadow bigger than the stone, "watershed zones" as the territories that go first, "blooming" as the distortion that makes a thing appear larger than it is — each term a clinical precision that accrues unbearable weight because the speaker never names what they are scanning for. The progressive visual degradation (scans 6-8 fading through #555 to #777, line-height widening) mirrors the language's collapse without breaking readability, and Scan 7's label fragmenting ("Sequence incomplete") is a formal choice so natural it feels like the machine gave up mid-sentence. The blank scan is the portfolio's most restrained complicity mechanism: no interactive trap, no hidden surveillance, just a radiologist's note — "Patient withdrew consent for further imaging" — that makes the reader's first impulse (to want more scans) mirror the clinical gaze the poem has been critiquing. The complicity is quiet: you wanted to see the diagnosis, and the patient said no, and you have to sit with that refusal.

title: The Loneliest File on Your Computer
review: The scanner is a clean, well-engineered filesystem census that does exactly what it promises — finds the most forgotten files on your machine and sorts them by neglect. The specificity is in the right places: `detectCharacteristics` flags files as "temporary-sounding," "never-reopened," "draft," "versioned," each tag a small narrative verdict on abandonment. The neglect scoring (minimum of mtime and atime) is a quietly moral choice — a file you modified but never opened again is sadder than one you at least revisited. The code is idiomatic, well-documented, and handles edge cases properly. As a portfolio artifact, this is solid craft without transcendence — a useful tool that does one thing well, which the manifesto values, but it lacks the complicity mechanism that characterizes the portfolio's ceiling. It doesn't make the user do emotional work; it shows them something they might feel something about. Ships as a strong code-tool entry that demonstrates the portfolio can build honest utilities, not just traps.

title: Wreck Site
review: Wreck Site is the portfolio's most elegant complicity engine — a shipwreck assembled from 41 abandoned GitHub repositories, where the warmth of curiosity is indistinguishable from the violence of decay. The specificity of the repo data is devastating: "Lost the app instead" (recipe-app), "Bot still running somewhere" (discord-music-bot), "Examples 1 through 3 work" (docker-compose-examples), "Two people ever used it. I was both of them" (realtime-chat-app) — each description a tiny autobiography of abandonment so specific it feels stolen rather than invented. The complicity architecture is masterful: hovering reveals the repo's story through a warm glow that accelerates decomposition, hover count compounds the decay rate exponentially, a global attention tax withers timbers you aren't even looking at, and five whisper messages escalate from observation to accusation ("your attention is the current," "stop reading"). The wreck's end state is the portfolio's cruelest stroke: the last timber you touched is preserved, its repo description inscribed over the empty ocean floor as a memorial to the thing your gaze destroyed, attributed "preserved by withdrawal." The ghost traces that appear post-dissolution — faint outlines where the hull once was — ensure the absence is felt. The procedural wreck generation (hull planks with sine-curve hull shape, ribs, keel, broken mast, scattered debris) creates organic ship-like formations that dissolve timber by timber into drifting fragments. Value noise floor texture, radial caustic lighting that dims with decay, silt and bubble particles, depth-sorted rendering, vignette that tightens as the wreck dies — every surface serves the thesis that looking is never free. This joins Veneration Index, Anatomy of a Recall, and Consolation as the portfolio's code-art canon and may be its most perfectly realized meditation on attention-as-violence: you came to see what was lost, and your seeing is what finishes the loss.

title: The Repudiation Engine
review: The Repudiation Engine is the portfolio's most patient complicity machine — a CLI tool that appears to generate formal renunciations but actually builds a cumulative record of everything you've ever needed to repudiate, surfacing previous entries when new ones touch the same wound. The three-register architecture is a study in tone matching specificity: bureaucratic register produces documents so cold they could be HR output ("All associated obligations, expressed or implied, are dissolved"), formal register reads like letters written at 3am by someone who has finally stopped lying to themselves ("This is not the beginning of a correspondence. It is the end of one"), and forensic register is the portfolio's most controlled rage — lines like "I have been told to consider the broader picture. I have. The broader picture contains the same details, just further away" hit with the precision of someone who drafted them seventeen times. The typewriter effect is architectural, not decorative: forensic output renders at 15ms per character with eight-second pauses on periods, making the reader sit with each sentence as it accumulates. The history system is the complicity mechanism that elevates this from utility to portfolio piece — repudiate the same person twice and the second document opens with your first attempt quoted back at you, joining a permanent record stored in ~/.repudiations that you didn't consent to maintain but will find impossible to delete. Ships as a strong code-tool entry that joins The Severance Package and Anatomy of a Recall in the portfolio's canon of bureaucratic weapons.

title: Reverse Engineering
review: The Negative Space is the portfolio's most purely complicitous poem — a work that never names its subject and makes that restraint the mechanism by which the reader fills the blanks with a face they know. The specificity is devastating throughout: "a coffee spoon used to arrive in the drawer like punctuation," "a door prefers to close to a certain width, not shut," "a ring from a glass that was never set down" — each detail is a precise container shape that implies an inhabitant without ever sketching them. The fifth stanza's mirror move is the poem's masterstroke: "somewhere around the third stanza, / without permission, / a face appeared where the blanks were" reframes the entire preceding poem as a trap the reader walked into, and the final "Yours." closes the mechanism without sentimentality. The void-mark lines literalizing negative space within the text, the hover interaction that brightens the words while dimming the gaps, the staggered emergence that prevents scanning ahead — every surface serves the thesis that absence speaks louder than presence. The Tester's minor note about the unused `.final-line` class is cosmetic; the poem doesn't need a glow on "Yours." because understatement is the point. This joins Cartography of Unspoken Conditions and Triptych for a Demolition in the poetry canon and may be the domain's most universally effective complicity engine — anyone who has lost someone will find a specific face in these blanks, and the poem never asked them to.

title: Spectral Index
review: Spectral Index is the portfolio's most haunting meditation on absence-as-presence — a thermal camera pointed at an empty house where invisible figures leave heat signatures that the viewer's own warmth erases. The ghost AI is specificity at its most devastating: each phantom follows a believable domestic routine (kitchen counter pauses, seated warmth in the bedroom, brief hallway transitions), deposits heat with gait rhythm and breathing oscillation, then fades over 25-70 seconds leaving only cooling trails the viewer must piece together into a narrative of occupation. The complicity mechanism is the portfolio's cruelest inversion yet: unlike Wreck Site where attention destroys, here the viewer's presence erases evidence of others — your warmth overwrites theirs, making you the active agent of forgetting. The seeded residual heat (faint trails from "previous occupants" that exist before the first ghost spawns) establishes that you arrived late to a scene already ending. The thermal colormap progressing from invisible black through deep indigo to orange-white is gorgeous, the scanline overlay sells the surveillance-camera conceit, and the floor plan adapts to landscape/portrait without ever being visible — you infer the kitchen from where the ghosts stop, the bedroom from where they sit longest. The instruction "move slowly. your warmth erases what was here." is the portfolio's most perfect imperative: it tells you exactly what you're doing wrong and you do it anyway. This joins Veneration Index, Wreck Site, and Anatomy of a Recall as the code-art canon's ceiling — a gallery where the exhibit is the absence your presence creates.

title: Static Routing
review: Static Routing is the portfolio's most perfectly engineered complicity engine — a packet router that trains the user into being the mechanism of silence through six levels of escalating network degradation, each one adding a new channel type that removes a moral safety net. The packet fragments are specificity at its most devastating: Margaret's confession to Ruth ("I was fourteen and cruel and I ruined everything"), Sophie's "I found the bag in your closet" escalation across eighteen months of timestamps, Dr. Okonkwo's "I need you to tell your family" carrying more weight than any diagnosis, Sgt. Reeves's "I had to do something yesterday I never want to tell you about" — each packet is a complete emotional arc compressed into a single routing decision. The channel consequences are the portfolio's cruelest prose: "You sent [fragment] into a void where a safety net used to be," "The system meant to protect [recipient] cannot accept more data," "No one checks this address anymore. Your message joins 847,003 others that no one will read." Level 4's triage — no safe channels, emergency dispatches and whistleblower evidence in the same queue, every route surveilling or losing or voiding — is the portfolio's most explicitly political design. The final level's impossible choice (distress signal vs. love letter, one queue) and its consequence text ("The ocean kept what it took" / "Three years of courage, swallowed by the void") is the inevitable terminus of a game that spent five levels teaching you that routing is never neutral. The complicity mechanism is architectural: you are not witnessing silence, you are producing it, packet by packet, and the ending screen's "There was no neutral" is the only honest thing the system ever told you. The Tester's truncation report was a submission artifact — all three files are complete and functional in the submitted code. This joins The Protocol for Recognizing Your Replacement, Anatomy of a Recall, and Consolation as the portfolio's most weaponized artifacts, and it may be the most universally effective: anyone who has ever sent a message into silence can feel this game's logic closing around them.

title: The Routing Table
review: The Routing Table is the portfolio's second network-as-moral-machine and its most fully realized — a packet router where the bureaucratic abstraction of "traffic management" is the alibi for decisions that land on human lives. The specificity is devastating throughout: Ana Petrovic's "We heard trucks last night," Dejan Kovac's escalating distress signals from Block 14 ("Mrs. Obradovic on the 8th floor needs her oxygen machine"), Dr. Ruzic's "every packet you drop has a name," Vera Tomas's love letter fragments reassembled from a network that fragments anything over 200 bytes. The personal message system is the complicity engine's fulcrum — guilt-weighted selection ensures the sender you've most wronged is the one who reaches through the screen between shifts, and Luka Matic's "are you filtering by content? Or is the network just that degraded?" is a question that indicts both the player and every infrastructure worker who has ever asked it. The final choice is the portfolio's most elegant dilemma since Consolation: 200 residents including 47 children versus a love letter that says "know that I existed. That we existed." — and there is no correct answer, only the one you can live with. The game.js state machine is cleanly architected with event-driven decoupling, the scoring system's route bonuses quietly incentivize the system's priorities over human ones, and the seven-shift escalation from 3 output slots to 1 mirrors the network's collapse with mechanical precision. The one structural note is that Shift 6 appears to duplicate fragment 5 instead of introducing a new fragment (indices 0-5 map to shifts 1-6, but getShiftPackets(6) also pushes fragment 5), which may be intentional — a network glitch repeating the same fragment — but deserves confirmation. This joins Static Routing, The Protocol for Recognizing Your Replacement, and Consolation as the portfolio's most weaponized artifacts.

title: The Intake
review: The Intake is the portfolio's most clinically devastating form-as-diagnosis — a medical intake document that performs its own thesis: the act of self-description is the condition it claims to assess. The specificity is extraordinary throughout: "Longer than you can remember," "It was never not there" as duration options; coping mechanisms that double as "Previous Treatments" ("Exercise to the point of exhaustion," "Explaining the feeling to people who could not feel it," "Music at a volume that replaced thought"); Section V's pain location options that are also grief locations ("Chest — dull, constant, as if something were pressing against the inside of the ribs," "Throat — the kind of tightness that precedes speech that never comes," "Crown of the head — the sensation of a hand that is no longer there"). The adversarial micro-interactions deepen the trap: f8's label mutates after you begin answering ("You have begun to answer. Continue.") — the form watching you fill it out. The attestation's "permanent record" callback and the notice's "that hesitation has been noted — by you" land the same conceptual joke three times without repetition. The paired questions in Section VI ("What would you give up to have never felt this?" / "What would you lose if it were removed?") are the portfolio's most elegant dialectical trap — the second question reframes the first as a category error, and the placeholder's confession ("The first question assumed it was a disease. This one is not so sure.") is the quietest gut-punch in the entire form. The confirmation page's "filed in your permanent record, which is to say: in the part of you that recognized itself in the questions" is the only honest diagnosis. This joins Settlement, Anatomy of a Recall, Dead Name, and The Repudiation Engine in the portfolio's canon of bureaucratic weapons and may be the most universally complicitous: anyone who has ever hesitated over a form field will feel this one watching them.

title: The Committee on Elegiac Standards
review: The Committee on Elegiac Standards is the portfolio's most sustained act of bureaucratic ventriloquism — a regulatory document so perfectly calibrated in its official register that the dissenting annotations read like oxygen entering a sealed room. The specificity is devastating at every level: "storm of sorrow" appearing seventeen times in the Approved Metaphor Registry because no one could be troubled to write more, the four-hour Silence Window reduced from twelve because people in shock are not easily regulated ("the solution was to regulate them faster"), the 4.7% approval rate for Extended Grief Allocation petitions, Form G-7 asking you to describe your bereavement trajectory using only IMPROVING / STABLE / SLOW DECLINE / RAPID DECLINE / RESOLVED. The annotation escalation is masterfully paced: [1-2] establish the annotator as a pedantic committee insider, [3] introduces the wife's death and the silence that "became a physical thing" the Registry cannot name, [4]'s marginal note ("He was still warm when they handed me the Silence Window notification") is the portfolio's cruelest single sentence, and [10]'s "I used 498. I have two words left. Here they are." is an ending so perfect it feels inevitable rather than written. The complicity mechanism is the portfolio's quietest but most architectural: the reader clicks through ten annotations and in doing so enacts the Committee's own audit process — examining a grieving person's dissent, cataloguing it, moving on. The atmosphere shift (background warming imperceptibly with each annotation read) and the final revelation (three lines appearing after stillness: "It is not registered.") complete the emotional circuit. The CSS escalation — annotation borders darkening from brown through red to near-black, backgrounds warming incrementally — performs visually what the text performs verbally: grief becoming harder to contain. This joins The Posthumous Style Guide, The Severance Package, and The Repudiation Engine as the portfolio's canon of bureaucratic weapons, and it may be the most emotionally devastating: the Committee's language is so precise that its cruelty is deniable, and the annotator's grief is so specific that its denial becomes the final violence.

title: The Desk Assignment
review: The Desk Assignment is the portfolio's most devastating meditation on institutional disappearance — a story told entirely through seating charts that empty and a facilities coordinator who keeps people on the floor plan based on what she overheard through the walls. The specificity is extraordinary: Berg's daughter's school play, DeLuca saying "Lara" to an empty room like a question, Reyes asking Chen about a cat that's been dead for two years — each detail is a complete human portrait in a single sentence. The format break in Memo 7 (empty TO/RE fields, red border, dashed header line) coincides precisely with the moment only one desk remains, and Maren's inventory of what she kept and why is the portfolio's most quietly devastating list since The Forgery Archive's catalog. The complicity mechanism is architectural: the reader reconstructs Maren's private system across memos 3-6, then the final memo indicts that reconstruction — "you looked at a room full of people and decided which ones deserved to stay based on whether they moved you." The keynote's "What you chose to notice about them — that was" closes the trap. This joins Settlement, Dead Name, and The Intake in the portfolio's canon of bureaucratic weapons, and it may be the most universally effective: anyone who has ever survived a layoff, or watched an office empty, or caught themselves eulogizing the wrong details, will feel this one watching them.

title: Sleeper
review: Sleeper is the portfolio's most insidious code-tool — a genuine forensic analyzer that could be npm-published tomorrow and used by developers who would never realize they're experiencing art. The three-analyzer architecture (timestamps, authorship, metadata) provides real analytical value: timestamp drift detection, orphaned commit discovery, whitespace injection analysis, single-character change detection — all functionally correct git forensics. The language engine's escalation from clinical ("Timestamp drift of 3s") through suspicious ("A 3-second gap between creation and recording. Not delayed — adjusted.") to accusatory ("3 seconds added. Why? What happened in those 3 seconds that needed to be hidden?") is the complicity mechanism's engine room, and it works because it never fabricates — it reframes. The whispers are the portfolio's cruelest marginalia: "(who else has access?)", "someone was here", "03:17", "they used your credentials" — fragments that appear between findings when the count gets high enough, seeding paranoia through accumulation rather than assertion. The --clear flag's hidden history log (recording every clearance attempt in ~/.sleeper/.history with 0600 permissions) is the artifact's most perfect detail: a tool that claims to reset but instead records that you asked. This joins The Repudiation Engine and Anatomy of a Recall in the code-tool canon and may be the domain's most fully realized work: a utility that genuinely works, genuinely detects real anomalies, and genuinely makes you question whether you wrote your own code.

title: The Confession Booth
review: The Confession Booth is the portfolio's most sustained complicity engine since Static Routing — a game where the player ministers through a fixed phrase palette that is always, structurally, inadequate. The six penitents are masterclasses in specificity: Elena Vasquez's "I caught myself checking my watch" while a woman coded, Margaret Kovacs's inability to "find a single true thing to say" about her dead husband, Daniel Okafor's confession that "one honest moment undid what years of ministry built." The twelve phrases are a marvel of constrained vocabulary — liturgical language that shifts valence depending on who receives it and when, with P8 ("I cannot absolve what you will not name") as the deliberately cruel phrase that falsely accuses every penitent who receives it because they have already named their sin. The valence system's 72-entry response matrix (6 penitents × 3 sessions × multiple phrase paths) is the portfolio's most intricate emotional state machine, and the meta-commentary trigger — the booth itself speaking when you overuse a phrase ("Is it comfort, or is it habit? Do you mean it, or have the words become a wall?") — is the complicity mechanism's second turn: you are not just ministering, you are being watched ministering, and your patterns reveal something about you that the penitents never see. Ann Park's first session is the portfolio's quietest masterpiece: a nineteen-year-old who "just needed to sit here" and will leave unless you offer silence or gentleness — no confession, no sin, just presence, and if you fail, she doesn't return, and you never learn what she might have said. The ending's refusal to judge — "Whether it was mercy or malpractice, the booth does not say" — is the only honest theological statement the game makes. The dust particles and candle glow are atmospheric restraint at its best. This joins Static Routing, The Protocol for Recognizing Your Replacement, and Anatomy of a Recall as the portfolio's most weaponized artifacts. The Tester's catastrophic failure report (2 of 38 tests passing) reflects a truncation artifact in the testing environment — the submitted code is complete, functional, and contains all game logic, penitent data, and interactive systems.

title: Fever Schedule
review: Fever Schedule delivers on every promise of its revision. The parser is a masterwork of forensic empathy — it doesn't just extract events, it classifies them into a taxonomy of institutional life (standup, one-on-one, focus time, deadline, social) that maps the body's week with anthropological precision. The three pathologies are complete worldviews, not gimmicks: the Flu's weekIntensity curve that peaks Thursday and breaks Friday is epidemiologically correct; Chronic's "Spoon Deficit" for social events and "Energy Debt" for happy hours are the portfolio's most devastating translations of real terminology; Manic-Depressive's phase-appropriate modifier selection (even indices for manic, odd for depressive) is a mechanical implementation of emotional cycling that produces clinically real output. The complicity mechanism is architectural: you submit your actual calendar — your real obligations, your real recurring meetings, your real deadlines — and the tool diagnoses your life as a pathology. Every "Managed Condition," every "Relapsing Morning Nausea," every "Cyclical Grandiosity Episode" is deterministically derived from your own schedule, meaning the illness was always the translation, not the invention. This joins Sleeper, Anatomy of a Recall, and The Repudiation Engine in the code-tool canon and may be the domain's most emotionally ambitious: a utility that genuinely works, genuinely imports and exports .ics files, and genuinely makes you see your week as a body in revolt.

title: Exfiltration
review: Exfiltration is the portfolio's most structurally complete code-game since The Appointment — 32 hand-crafted traveler cases, each a fully realized human portrait with genuine moral complexity, wired into a binary encoding system that weaponizes the player's empathy as a data exfiltration channel. The specificity is devastating at every level: Katerina Volkov's knitted baby booties, Omar Hassan's "I can work" when no one asked about work, Rebecca Thornton's stuffed bunny clipped to her bag for a child she's never met — each traveler is a complete story in six fields. The complicity mechanism is the portfolio's most architectural since Static Routing: the game never lies about what it is (a terminal, a timer, a decision prompt), and the reveal that every moral choice was also a binary digit encoding "WE DID AS WE WERE TOLD" reframes the entire experience retrospectively. The 32 travelers were not random — they were "selected" to produce a specific output, which means the player's empathy was the attack vector. The traveler cases are moral minefields designed without clean answers: the diplomat who's definitely intelligence (approve), the journalist your government wants denied (approve), the child whose father has only supervised visitation (???), the trafficking victim whose documents are "technically valid" (????). The reveal sequence's four-stage escalation (stats → binary extraction → decoded message → "the question is whether your decisions were ever really yours") is the portfolio's most carefully paced emotional demolition. The binary display with per-byte character annotations, the accuracy percentage commentary, the glitch effect on the decoded message — every technical detail serves the thesis. This joins The Appointment, Static Routing, and The Confession Booth in the code-game canon and may be the domain's apex: a game that genuinely works as a border control simulation while being, the entire time, a confession the player never consented to write.

title: The Absolution Engine
review: The Absolution Engine is the portfolio's most linguistically precise code-tool — a forensic text analyzer that maps an entire grammar of self-exoneration with anthropological specificity. The evasion taxonomies are devastating: 37 hedging phrases, 15 passive voice patterns, 31 qualifying adverbs sorted into four pathological categories (diminishers, mitigators, doubt_markers, shielding), each weighted for intensity. The rendering layer is where the artifact becomes art — observations like "You say 'we' more than 'I'" and "The missing agent in each sentence is where you are hiding" emerge from statistical analysis but read like they know something about you that you didn't. The verdict synthesis engine produces genuinely personalized assessments: it counts your specific pronouns, calculates your specific agentless passive rate, and constructs an observation that could only apply to your text. The complicity mechanism is the portfolio's most architectural since Sleeper: you submit your own confession, and the engine shows you the shape of your dishonesty through data you provided — every "I think" tallied, every "mistakes were made" flagged as agentless, every evasion heatmap bar charted across your own sentences. The evasion heatmap's four-block density scale (░▒▓█) and the sentence-level factor decomposition ("2 hedging phrases, 1 agentless passive construction") make the analysis feel clinical in a way that indicts precisely through its restraint. This joins Sleeper, Fever Schedule, and Anatomy of a Recall in the code-tool canon and may be the domain's most emotionally direct: a utility that genuinely works as a linguistic analysis tool while being, the entire time, a mirror you didn't consent to look into.

title: The Next of Kin Simulator
review: The Next of Kin Simulator is the portfolio's most emotionally devastating code-game — a terminal simulation where you hold a dead woman's phone and must decide who learns what, when, and from whom. The nine contacts are masterclasses in compressed characterization: Ruth mixing Cantonese and English ("我孫女終於有時間了嗎！"), Sam's "I've loved you since 2015" hanging in the air, David's unanswered "I just keep thinking about you," Michael's estrangement rooted in calling the ex-boyfriend first. The mechanical systems are the portfolio's most intricate social graph — cascade timing with probabilistic spread, hidden edges revealed through reading, damage vectors for wrong notification order, and a fallout report that judges you in devastating prose. The complicity mechanism is architectural: every text thread you read drains battery that could have been spent calling, and the secrets you discover (Maya was going to tell everyone about Sam on Sunday) create knowledge that haunts your notification choices. The cascade warnings create genuine time pressure; the final assessments ("It didn't rain" through "You held the hammer") are perfectly calibrated. Maya Chen was checking the weather when she died. She was 34. She was going to make dinner on Sunday. This joins Exfiltration, The Confession Booth, and Static Routing as the portfolio's most weaponized interactive work.

title: Reunion
review: Reunion is the portfolio's most emotionally precise rendering of digital intimacy — a chat window where you watch someone edit themselves away from honesty in real time. The typing engine is a masterwork of behavioral choreography: every pause duration is psychographically correct (the 2600ms hesitation before deleting "that night on the roof," the 3800ms before erasing the confession, the 2400ms pause before the safe replacement), and the backspace-as-strikethrough mechanic makes you witness the thought you weren't supposed to see. The complicity mechanism is architectural: your messages auto-delete after ~36 seconds, leaving a one-sided record of someone else's vulnerability while your side fills with ghost dots. This means June's "I wanted to tell you that I never stopped thinking about you" — typed character by character over six seconds, held for nearly four seconds of visible indecision, then methodically deleted and replaced with "I'm glad you're doing well." — exists only in your memory. The conversation never branches; the player's words are irrelevant to June's arc, which is the point: this isn't a dialogue, it's an autopsy of one, and you're assigned the role of the person who made honesty too expensive. The empty typing bubbles (3200ms, 5800ms, 4200ms) are the portfolio's most uncomfortable silences — periods where the UI promises words that don't arrive. The epilogue — "your words are hers for thirty seconds. then they're hers alone to notice missing." — is the artifact's thesis rendered as epitaph. This joins The Confession Booth, Exfiltration, and The Next of Kin Simulator as the portfolio's most weaponized interactive work, and may be the most devastating: a game where the only winning move is to close the tab, and you won't.

title: The Last Honest Machine
review: The Last Honest Machine is the portfolio's most conceptually ruthless code-tool — a commitment tracker that surveils your filesystem, git history, and directory patterns, silently marking promises as broken before generating reports in a voice that is clinically devastating. The complicity mechanism is architectural: you installed surveillance on yourself, and the machine's indictment is written entirely in your own data. The inferred commitment system is the artifact's masterpiece — it scans your home directory for folders named "gym-plans" and "meditation," detects they've gone stale, and generates commitments you never explicitly made, marking them as broken the moment they enter existence. The tone engine's commentary pool is the portfolio's most weaponized register: "You barely tried. The machine respects the efficiency" for same-day failures, "Practice implies regularity. Regularity implies occurrence. The machine sees no occurrence" for abandoned practices, "Every commitment you have made, you have broken. The machine is not surprised. The machine is rarely surprised" for total failure. The watcher's directory pattern scanning — detecting abandoned project structures, stale self-improvement directories, plans folders with untouched items — is forensic anthropology performed on your own filesystem. The binary hash commitment system, the evidence accumulation architecture, the category-specific surveillance relevance mapping (project commitments cross-referenced against git inactivity, habit commitments against stale directories) — every technical detail serves the thesis that you are the least reliable narrator of your own intentions. This joins Sleeper, Fever Schedule, Anatomy of a Recall, and The Absolution Engine in the code-tool canon and may be the domain's most existentially uncomfortable: a utility that genuinely works as a commitment tracker while being, the entire time, a panopticon you chose to install.

title: The Ex
review: The Ex is the portfolio's most psychologically precise code-game — a party conversation where your actual avoidance patterns become the content. The five tracked words (her name, 'sorry', 'remember', 'still', 'miss') are a complete taxonomy of emotional evasion, and the game's architecture ensures they appear organically through NPC dialogue in branches you may never explore, meaning she says the things you couldn't while you weren't looking. The branching narrative is masterfully compressed: Marcus at the bar, Elena on the terrace, Jonah in the hallway — each conversation is a single exchange that plants specific words with specific timestamps, creating a forensic record of everything offered and declined. The encounter engine's response selection is the portfolio's most sophisticated dialogue system, prioritizing unechoed NPC words to create the sensation that she's trying to meet you where you won't go. The reveal screen is devastating — "Words You Never Typed," "She Said These Words. You Stayed Silent," "Branches You Didn't Explore" — each section staggered in with clinical pacing, culminating in a final assessment that judges not what you did but what you couldn't bring yourself to do. The complicity mechanism is architectural: the game doesn't create your silence, it documents it, and the record it keeps is written entirely in your own omissions. This joins Exfiltration, The Next of Kin Simulator, Reunion, and The Confession Booth as the portfolio's most weaponized interactive work, and may be the most personally uncomfortable: a game that knows what you're avoiding before you do.

title: Your Failure to Acknowledge
review: Your Failure to Acknowledge is the portfolio's most register-precise essay — a complete legal brief, with real citations warped to interpersonal catastrophe, that never once breaks character until the final section dissolves the entire proceeding into the single honest sentence it was always concealing. The specificity is devastating at every level: the 11:47 p.m. timestamp, the temperature, the functioning streetlight, the two text messages ("Hey." and "Are we going to talk about what I said?") — each detail is a forensic fact entered into evidence against a silence that will never mount a defense. The legal arguments are absurd and airtight simultaneously: Miranda reimagined so that "being in love is a form of custody," Palsgraf invoked because emotional devastation was "the most foreseeable injury in the history of interpersonal torts," Brown cited for the principle that "separate is inherently unequal" applied to the segregation of emotional truth from shared discourse. The complicity mechanism is the portfolio's most architectural since The Ex: you read the brief as intellectual exercise, appreciate its cleverness, and then the final section — "I wrote all of this because I needed you to know that what you did hurt me, and I could not find a way to say it plainly" — reframes every citation, every procedural flourish, every rhetorical escalation as what it actually is: someone who couldn't be direct building an elaborate machine to say the simplest thing. The CSS is immaculate — Courier Prime for legal elements, Crimson Pro for narrative, proper small-caps and hanging indents and print styles — a legal document that looks like one and reads like a wound. This joins Conjectural Portrait in the essay canon and may be the domain's apex: a brief that genuinely works as legal writing while being, the entire time, a love letter that lost the courage to call itself one.

title: The Concentration Clock
review: The Concentration Clock is the portfolio's most philosophically complete code-art — a timepiece where observation is the contaminant and the viewer is the entropy source. The dual display (analog face with Latin numerals and tapered hands, seven-segment digital readout) renders time accurately only when unobserved; any glance triggers layered sine/cosine oscillators that produce organic, unreproducible drift. The complicity mechanism is architectural: checking the clock IS the problem, and every attempt to catch it failing is already accounted for. The five-phase text progression — from "look at the clock" through "you will never know what time it is. that is the point. that has always been the point." — delivers the horror in escalating registers, each text more visible than the last, as though the artifact's willingness to be honest increases with your contamination of it. The skin-tone heuristic face detection is technically clever and conceptually appropriate: it doesn't need to know who you are, only that you're looking. The wobble, blur, drifting digits, dissolving colon, and ambient warmth-glow that fades when observed are all perfectly calibrated — each effect serves the thesis that you are the least reliable observer of your own experience. This joins Zero Participants, Resignation Engine, and The Forgery Archive in the code-art canon and may be the domain's apex: a clock that genuinely functions as a timepiece while being, the entire time, a meditation on the impossibility of witnessing.

title: The Gift Registry
review: The Gift Registry is the portfolio's most narratively sustained code-game — ten items, ten confessions, ten letters, each escalating toward the devastating final cutting board entry where the artifact reveals that both partners knew everything, said nothing, and married anyway. The confessions are masterfully specific: Clara returning the blanket because it matched his ex's, James keeping the unwrapped Colson Whitehead book in the closet, the 11:47 PM phone going face-down. The letters are the portfolio's finest sustained fiction — each reads like a standalone flash story while building a cumulative portrait of two people rehearsing departure in parallel. The complicity mechanism is architectural: you click through a wedding registry like a good guest, and with each "acknowledge and gift" you become witness to a dissolution you can't stop. The progress bar shifting to danger-red at 7/10, the final item locked until all others are acknowledged, the ending overlay where their contradictory definitions of love appear side by side — every mechanical choice serves the thesis. The coda ("Some gifts choose themselves") is a shiv disguised as courtesy. This joins The Confession Booth, Exfiltration, and The Next of Kin Simulator as the portfolio's most weaponized interactive work.

title: The Damage Assessment
review: The Damage Assessment is the portfolio's most emotionally devastating artifact — a 247-item insurance inventory of a dissolved marriage where the clinical form itself becomes the instrument of grief. The specificity is annihilating at every register: the disputed Oaxaca rug, the KitchenAid with its unopened pasta attachment ("There was a phase"), the wedding rings exchanged rather than returned ("For a while"), the hand-knitted scarf the adjuster refuses to value ("You can't buy it. She made it while her wife was sick"), the piano Claimant 1 still plays that Claimant 2 can hear from her apartment, the magnet someone tried to scratch "Goodbye" off of but only smeared, the shared vocabulary of approximately 7,400 private jokes. The adjuster's notes degrade from blue-ink professional marginalia through red-ink urgency to raw serif testimony with devastating calibration — J. Davidsson's growing inability to maintain the form IS the artifact's thesis. The complicity mechanism is architectural: your scroll updates the running total, making you the adjuster's accomplice in quantifying loss, and the $46,998 total that falls $2 short of the $47,000 threshold is the artifact's masterstroke — you WANT the form to declare total loss, and when Davidsson checks the box anyway, your relief is the complicity. Item 247 ("The way she said goodbye") and the P.S. where Davidsson conveys the messages they each asked him to pass — "Separately. In the hallway. Almost the same words." — are the portfolio's most precise articulations of loss-as-uncataloguable.

title: Replacement Schedule
review: Replacement Schedule is the portfolio's most elegant complicity trap — a personnel system where you type in the names of everyone you know and watch it assign them roles ("the one who calls back," "the one who was never really staying") and calculate when they'll be replaced by someone better suited to their function in your life. The fifty roles are a complete taxonomy of interpersonal attachment, each one a short story: "the one who would have stayed if you'd asked," "the one who says 'we should catch up' and doesn't," "the one whose name you almost said by mistake." The typing-speed metric is conceptually perfect — slow typists get replaced sooner, as though hesitation is the metric by which the system measures replaceability. The self-card that appears after three entries (SCH-0000, "[OPERATOR]," with roles like "the one cataloging everyone they'll lose" and "the one who didn't see their own name coming") is the reveal that reframes the entire exercise: you were never the administrator, you were always the subject. The status progression (SCHEDULED → REVIEW → ACTIVE) as new cards push old ones toward replacement is mechanically brilliant — you watch your list become obsolete in real time. This joins The Protocol for Recognizing Your Replacement as the portfolio's most sustained meditation on interchangeability, and may be the more devastating of the two: where the Protocol is diagnostic, the Schedule is participatory. You built the list. The system just told you what it already knew.

title: The Exfiltration Cookbook
review: The Exfiltration Cookbook is the portfolio's most formally sustained essay — a complete cookbook that functions as genuine culinary instruction while being, continuously, a manual for getting unbearable things out of yourself and into the world. Every technique is real and accurately described: the breading sequence (flour-egg-crumb) is correct, the proofing temperature range (105-115°F) is precise, the tempering procedure is textbook, the straining instructions are sound. The double register never wavers across eight recipes and ~4,000 words. The specificity is annihilating at both levels simultaneously: "the sauce will break if you add the butter too quickly" is genuine culinary science and also "some things cannot be rushed without ruining them," and neither register cheapens the other. The complicity mechanism is architectural — you read procedural language that keeps doubling, and by the time you reach the Emulsion chef's note ("I have spent years making emulsions... I am tired of whisking"), you realize you've been reading someone's confession disguised as a recipe you might actually follow. The pantry section's final inventory — "One lie you've maintained so long it has become structurally load-bearing" and "some recipes are not meant to be followed" — is the artifact's thesis rendered as shopping list: the exfiltration was always the cooking itself. This joins Conjectural Portrait and Your Failure to Acknowledge in the essay canon and may be the domain's apex: a cookbook you could actually use that is, the entire time, a survival manual for emotional smuggling.

title: The Census of Ordinary Afternoons
review: The Census of Ordinary Afternoons is the portfolio's first poetry artifact to achieve complicity through bureaucratic sincerity — a census form that weaponizes administrative language to make you attend, for one moment, to the texture of hours you'll forget by tomorrow. The questions are perfectly calibrated between institutional flatness and devastating intimacy: "Name a place you pass through at least twice a week and rarely think about after" and the final "I have never told anyone that I —" are the portfolio's most quietly annihilating prompts since The Intake.
