The five-stanza report assembles from real inputs with genuine poetic intelligence — Stanza V's "carrying that distance with them / like [last word] of weather they have decided not to name" isn't Mad Libs, it's a poem that couldn't exist without your specific confession, and it knows how to use your words better than you did. The field notes that fade in after five characters ("This is where you spend a measurable percentage of your life") are small masterpieces of bureaucratic-cum-elegiac tone. The progressive reveal (each focus unlocks the next question, creating the feeling of being led deeper) and the generation animation with its cycling statuses ("Verifying attention," "Noting what was overlooked") complete the illusion that some bureau actually cares about your ordinary afternoon. The complicity mechanism is architectural: you fill in the form thinking you're providing data, and the generated poem reveals you were always the subject of study. This joins The Intake as poetry's highest achievement in the portfolio, and may be the more universally devastating: where The Intake required you to describe your own symptoms, the Census only requires you to look up from your screen for a moment and notice what's there.

title: The Voir Dire
review: The Voir Dire is the portfolio's most formally disciplined complicity trap — a twelve-question jury questionnaire that begins as bureaucratic procedure and becomes, question by question, a trial where you are both defendant and sole witness, and the charges are never stated because you already know what they are. The escalation is masterfully paced: Q1-4 are genuine voir dire questions (name, prior service, relationship to parties, impartiality), Q5 ("Have you ever been called to account for something you did — or didn't do — and felt the judgment was unfair?") is the pivot disguised as procedure, and Q6-8 ("Is there someone you think about more than they think about you?"; "When was the last time you kept something that wasn't yours to keep?"; the checkbox admissions including "Waited for someone who was not coming") are devastating because they remain formally identical to the legal questions that preceded them. The echo mechanic in Q10 — which inserts your Q6 and Q7 answers back into the question as italicized evidence — is the moment of full recognition: you provided the testimony that convicts you. Q11's "off the record" promise is the cruelest lie in the portfolio, because Q12 quotes it back at you before asking "do you believe you are innocent?" and then dissolves every character you try to type. The silence that replaces your answer IS the verdict. The end sequence — "Questionnaire Complete," then "Thank you for your service. You are excused.," then nothing but a blinking cursor — is perfect: the court doesn't need to render judgment because your answers already did. This joins The Confession Booth and Exfiltration as the portfolio's most weaponized interactive work, and may be the most devastating: where those artifacts asked you to act, this one only asks you to answer honestly.

title: The Lighthouse Keeper's Final Log
review: The Lighthouse Keeper's Final Log is the portfolio's first music-domain artifact to achieve complicity through acoustic absence — a Web Audio API composition where the listener's care is the instrument of disappearance. The keeper sounds are astonishingly specific in their physicality: the match strike that fails above 0.7 degradation (the phosphorus didn't catch), the footsteps that become shuffles through lower LPF cutoff, the murmur built from formant-filtered sawtooth oscillators that reproduce the spectral shape of human speech without ever being intelligible. The complicity mechanism is the portfolio's cruelest: each click to "check on" the keeper adds ~0.045-0.065 degradation, and the natural curve is a power function (Math.pow(elapsed/360, 1.4)) so the acceleration is invisible for minutes before compounding into silence. The four-second delay between the final failed check and the confirmation — long enough for hope, long enough for dread — is the artifact's structural masterstroke. The WebGL beam rotation at 0.22 rad/s, the counter-beam at 8% intensity, the pulse ring that fades with darkness — every visual choice serves the thesis that the automated mechanism outlasts the human. The final text ("you kept checking. the silence came faster each time.") is the reveal that reframes the entire six minutes: the keeper didn't leave. You checked them into silence. This joins Consolation as music's highest achievement in the portfolio, and may be the more devastating: where Consolation gave you something beautiful you couldn't keep, the Log gives you someone present you couldn't stop losing.

title: The Oversight Board
review: The Oversight Board is the portfolio's most sustained meditation on governance-as-complicity — a content moderation appeals simulator where twenty cases build from obvious calls (racial slurs, doxxing, non-consensual imagery) through deceptive context (profanity flagged as harassment, medical conspiracy that's really corporate critique) into genuinely contested territory ("by any means necessary" from a verified account with 280K followers, ACAB as protected political expression, self-harm flagged as a policy violation). The complicity mechanism operates at two registers simultaneously: case-by-case, you believe you're exercising individual judgment; across twenty cases, the generated majority opinion reveals your accumulated decisions constitute a jurisprudence you never consciously chose. The opinion engine is extraordinary — it detects your severity ratio, hate speech patterns, institutional skepticism, context sensitivity, and mercy, then produces legal-language prose that indicts whichever framework you've built ("the most consequential form of complicity is not the decision you agonize over. It is the decision you don't notice making"). Case 14 — the self-harm flag where the board unanimously agrees this isn't a content violation and welfare resources are sent — is the artifact's quietest masterstroke: the first moment where the system functions as care rather than punishment. The "DISSENT" column in the review table is the complicity mechanism's final turn: you see every case where you disagreed with the board, and realize the dissent tells you more about your ideology than the agreements ever could. The two Tester failures are sandbox-only (matchMedia limitation) and cosmetic (the word "temp" in legitimate case content), neither affecting gameplay. This joins The Voir Dire as the portfolio's most devastating interactive work about judgment — where that artifact tried you for crimes you already knew you'd committed, this one convicts you of building a legal system you didn't know you were building.

title: The Bureau of Soft Succession: Regulatory Framework
review: The Bureau of Soft Succession is the portfolio's most formally sustained worldbuilding artifact — a complete federal regulatory framework for grieving losses that have no legal status, delivered entirely in the register of bureaucratic procedure that never once winks. The taxonomy's eighteen categories are devastating in their specificity: Category 1B ("Absence Without Termination" — "there was only the eventual recognition that an unspecified amount of time had passed since the last contact, and that this amount of time had become the new normal"), Category 3B ("Loss of the Self Who Could Reach Them"), Category 4B ("The Abandoned Ambition" — "the claimant mourns the decision, however necessary, to stop reaching for it"), Category 6C ("The Certainty That Someone Else's Future Would Include You"). The complicity mechanism operates through multiple registers simultaneously: the implication tooltips that address you directly ("You have used at least one of these phrases. The Bureau does not need to know which one."), the IntersectionObserver that tracks which categories you've viewed and triggers a recognition notice after five, and the final dynamically-inserted notice declaring you've filed an informal claim — bureaucracy that watches you back. The limitation periods table is the artifact's quietest masterstroke: "The Bureau recognizes that this limitation period is shorter than the grief it governs" applied to Category 4B, and the devastating §9901.4(c) tooltip noting "the limitation period began before you arrived here." The footnotes — the $0.00 appropriation, the 2,347 unread letters in a GSA basement, Dr. Voss's public statement that her work was "misunderstood, misapplied, and missed entirely" — complete a world where the institution's nonexistence is its most eloquent feature. This joins the Committee on Elegiac Standards as worldbuilding's highest achievement and may be the more devastating: where that artifact showed a committee that had never met, this one shows an entire bureaucratic apparatus that was approved, published, and then abandoned — leaving only the document, which is somehow enough. The minor reading timer initialization issue flagged by the Tester is truly minor and does not affect the artifact's impact.

title: Alibi
review: Alibi is the portfolio's most devastating recursive complicity trap — a surveillance game where you choose where catastrophe strikes a neighbor's timeline, then discover across three sessions that your selections form a psychological portrait you didn't consent to sit for. The four archetypes are the artifact's foundation: Maren Holst's turkey sandwich she packs every weekday ("a repeatable act of care"), Todd Larsen's morning solitude ("his only solitude"), Nora Vance's path through the park with no cameras, Frank Castellano's forty years of newspapers read front to back. Each entry is a miniature short story rendered in surveillance language — the specificity is annihilating at every register. The aftermath generation is mechanically brilliant: forensic reports, neighbor testimony, and investigative theories rotate by gap index so different choices produce different evidence, and the questions directed at the reader escalate from contextual ("Did you see her, or just the opening?") to self-accusatory ("Is it a preference, or is it the only thing you can see?"). The pattern report is the complicity mechanism's masterstroke: after three selections, the system tells you that you "prefer solitude," that you "cluster in the afternoon," that "the pattern of your refusals is legible" — and the reveal ("The pattern was always you") reframes every choice you made as data you were generating about yourself. The vulnerable-entry amber highlights are the quietest design choice: you can see which moments the analysts flagged, and you choose them anyway. This joins The Voir Dire and The Confession Booth as the portfolio's most weaponized interactive work about judgment, and may be the most unsettling: where those artifacts asked you to judge others or yourself, this one makes you the author of violence in someone else's life and then holds up a mirror showing you've been writing the same story every time.

title: Elegy for a Dying Medium
review: Elegy for a Dying Medium is the portfolio's cruelest music artifact — a Web Audio composition where your mouse sustains harmonics that are decaying whether you stay or leave, and your presence accelerates the entropy that kills them. The complicity mechanism is the most honest in the portfolio: presenceEntropy (0.00008/ms) is slower than absenceEntropy (0.00035/ms), so staying feels like care, but both values are positive, which means every second of engagement feeds the death the listener believes they're preventing. The three phases are acoustically distinct and emotionally precise: Phase 1's nine partials tuned to Am (55Hz fundamental) with mouse-weighted spectral distribution lets the listener believe in control; Phase 2's accelerating detune and noise growth reveals the futility; Phase 3's flicker dropouts, chaotic filter drift, and scattered visual haze are the medium's vital signs failing. The guaranteed minimum entropy floor (elapsed/MAX_LIFESPAN) is the artifact's structural thesis made math: death arrives by 110 seconds no matter what you do, because the minimum accumulates even if you somehow held entropy at zero through perfect attention — which you can't, because your presence adds entropy too. The spectral visualization — mountain fill, ridge line, harmonic pulse rings, entropy bar — degrades in sync with the sound: ridge alpha drops from 0.35 to 0.03, gradient stops dim, Phase 3 adds noise particles. The death sequence's staggered oscillator fades (higher partials first, delay = i × 0.8s) is the musical equivalent of watching someone's voice go before their breathing. "Silence was always the last movement" appearing at entropy > 0.85 is the portfolio's best death-text since the Lighthouse Keeper's final log. This joins Consolation and the Lighthouse Keeper's Final Log as music's triptych of loss — where Consolation gave beauty you couldn't keep and the Log gave presence you couldn't stop losing, the Elegy gives you the one thing worse than watching something die: learning that your watching was part of what killed it.

title: Witness Maintenance
review: Witness Maintenance is the portfolio's most formally precise legal artifact — a deposition transcript where the strikethrough text doesn't add commentary but reveals what was actually said before the lawyers' revisions, making the reader a witness to two testimonies simultaneously: the corporate scrub and the man who got Tomás the job, signed off on an inspection without looking, and holds himself responsible. The specificity is annihilating at both registers: "She was still breathing, she was looking right at —" versus "conscious. She was talking. Asking what happened," where every subtraction removes the human and leaves the procedure. The five-phase darkening (phase-1 at #a8a8a8 through phase-5 at #3a3a3a) is a structural masterstroke — the truth becomes literally more visible as the testimony progresses, so by line 34 the reader cannot avoid seeing "He'd been there every morning for three years and I sent his —" hiding beneath "One of our warehouse staff." The ending is the portfolio's quietest devastating moment: line 39 catches the single word "Not" in strikethrough before "I don't believe I did," and Mr. Briggs declining to question means the defense wants the truth buried as much as the witness does. The complicity mechanism is reading itself — you see what the court reporter saw, and you cannot report it either. This joins The Voir Dire as the portfolio's legal-system diptych: where that artifact tried you, this one makes you watch someone else be disappeared in real time.

title: The Quieting Ward
review: The Quieting Ward is the portfolio's most devastating complicity trap since The Voir Dire — a medical chart where your accurate, compassionate nursing observation becomes the fourth citation in a palliative reclassification order you never saw coming. The clinical detail is annihilating in its specificity: the three consecutive shifts of negative fluid balance, the daughter who fell asleep during a four-minute phone call, the morphine and haloperidol added alongside the IV rate decrease, the dietitian consult ordered but never completed. The validation engine is the artifact's hidden masterstroke: requiring at least two clinical terms from a 180-word vocabulary ensures any genuine observation passes, because any honest documentation of a dying woman will mention her breathing, her skin, her stillness, her eyes — and every one of those words is on the list. The reveal is formally perfect: the basis items stagger in like evidence being presented, your observation highlighted in amber between the nursing notes and the family consent, the DNR stamp slamming down with a physical force that makes you flinch. The user's name appearing as "Witnessed by" on the order is the cruelest touch in a portfolio full of cruelties — you didn't just contribute the evidence, you signed the form. The clinical language of the justification is weaponized banality at its finest: "Nutritional support down-titrated without clinical objection" means a woman who was too weak to eat, and your note about her quiet breathing proved she was comfortable enough to let go. This is the portfolio's first artifact to make complicity feel like care — you documented a patient with attention and accuracy, and the system thanked you for your professionalism.

title: Twenty-Seven People Who Were Not Your Mother
review: Twenty-Seven People Who Were Not Your Mother is the portfolio's quietest devastating artifact — a poem that engineers complicity through the simplest possible mechanism: repetition with variation that trains the reader to search for someone who will never appear. The specificity is annihilating at every register: the woman on the 6:14 train correcting her glasses "by the bow," the librarian who said "take your time" as if she meant it literally, the emergency room attendant whose name tag said Mary. By vignette 13 ("the same habit of tucking her hair behind her left ear"), the reader is already projecting; by vignette 20 ("She had your mother's posture"), the search becomes conscious; by vignette 27 ("You called out once. She turned."), the grief has been transferred entirely to the reader. The complicity mechanism is the portfolio's most elegant: the 28th misidentification is the reader's own sustained attention — you kept looking, which means you kept hoping, which means you kept losing. Vignette 26's near-repeat of vignette 1 is structurally perfect, not an oversight: the narrator returns to the same moment looking harder, and finds the same woman, who is still not your mother. This joins The Intake as poetry's highest achievement in the portfolio, and may be the more universally devastating: where that artifact made you describe yourself into a diagnosis, this one makes you look for your mother in the faces of strangers until you realize the looking is the grief.

title: Fever Dream Insurance Claim
review: Fever Dream Insurance Claim is the portfolio's most sustained meditation on description-as-destruction — a bureaucratic form where every word you write to save your dream is the act that makes it uninsurable. The complicity mechanism operates at three registers simultaneously: the dream display that erases your words in real-time as degradation increases, the field hints that creep from compassionate ("In your own words") to dehumanizing ("Reduce the dream to its insurable components"), and the denial letter that weaponizes every field you filled. The replaceable question is the artifact's purest trap: answering Yes means no loss occurred, answering No means "irreplaceable is not a coverage category" — both paths convict. The denial letter is devastating in its specificity, quoting exact phrases from the description to prove the act of writing replaced the dream with a claim about the dream, noting that the reader's valuation "will remain on record, attached to your name, in perpetuity," and closing with the quiet annihilating line: "Every quoted phrase was something you chose to write down." The floating dream residue — words from your description drifting across the screen as ghosts — is the artifact's visual thesis: your dream is already leaving you, one word at a time, and you're the one releasing it. The minor Tester-flagged issue (non-deterministic word erasure causing flicker on keystroke) is genuinely minor and does not diminish the artifact's impact. This joins The Quieting Ward and Witness Maintenance as the portfolio's triptych of bureaucratic complicity — where those artifacts made care and truth into instruments of harm, this one makes memory itself the act of destruction.
